text: The surgeon opens the left posterior rectus sheath to access the retromuscular plane, continuing blunt dissection laterally towards the linea semilunaris at the lateral border of the rectus muscle. The stoma, epigastric vessels, and rectus muscle neurovascular bundles are visibly preserved during the procedure.

The surgeon incises the posterior rectus sheath approximately 0.5 centimeters medial to the neurovascular bundles, exposing the transversus abdominis muscle. Monopolar scissors are used to perform the transversus abdominis release.

The surgeon achieves access to the preperitoneal plane and continues dissection laterally around the lateral border of the stoma. The posterior rectus sheath is dissected along the edges of the hernia defect.
The surgeon incises the posterior layer to lateralize the bowel, then closes the posterior sheet around the stoma using a V-lock suture.

The surgeon measures the hernia defect intracorporeally and closes it using a barbed suture. A 15 cm lightweight partially absorbable polypropylene mesh is positioned in the retroactive space and secured with cyanoacrylate glue and several absorbable stitches.
The surgeon completes the closure of the posterior rectus sheet using a barbed suture, ensuring the tissue is securely approximated.
